about.headline: A Living Tradition, 143 Years in the Making
about.body: Since 1883, HGD Sveta Cecilija has stood as the guardian of Korčula's most treasured ritual. The Moreška — Europe's last authentic war dance — is performed here, on this island, by the sons of Korčula. Iron swords, archaic verse, and seven battles that have not changed in centuries. Come witness it.

history.headline: A Curated History
history.subline: Four moments that brought a Spanish ritual of mock combat to a Korčulan stone courtyard — and kept it there.

vignettes.title: The Dance Is Born
vignettes.body: The earliest recorded Moreška-style performance takes place in Lérida, commemorating the Christian capture of Aragon. A ritual of mock combat between two armies — one that will travel the Mediterranean for centuries before finding its final home.

vignettes.title: First Record in Korčula
vignettes.body: A town journal entry dated 7 March 1666 records a Moreška performance during carnival. The dance has already taken root. While similar traditions fade across Dalmatia, Korčula holds on — and never lets go.

vignettes.title: HGD Sveta Cecilija Is Founded
vignettes.body: Local citizens establish the Korčula Singing Society St. Cecily. Its founding statutes declare a mission to "ennoble the hearts of local youth" and defend Croatian language, song, and identity. The institutional home of Moreška is born.

vignettes.title: A New Era
vignettes.body: Following Croatia's declaration of independence, HGD Sveta Cecilija is officially re-established under its original name and mission. The society returns to its roots: preserving the Moreška and cultivating every dimension of Korčulan cultural life.

Guardians of the Steel Dance
since 1883.

aboutPage.heroHeadline: The Story of HGD Sveta Cecilija
aboutPage.heroSubtitle: Guardians of Korčula's living heritage since 1883
intro: On a small island off the Dalmatian coast, a cultural society has kept a 400-year-old sword dance alive through wars, occupations, and the rise and fall of nations. HGD Sveta Cecilija — founded in 1883 during the Croatian National Revival — is not merely a performance group. It is the institutional soul of Korčula, and the last guardian of a tradition that has survived everything history could throw at it.

The Moreška is performed every summer at the open-air Summer Cinema — not as a tourist spectacle, but as a living inheritance, passed from father to son, unchanged in its essential form for generations. The same iron swords. The same archaic Korčulan verse. The same seven battles.

Around the Moreška, HGD Sveta Cecilija has built four active ensembles: the Moreška troupe, the Wind Orchestra, Klapa Sveta Cecilija, and the Choir. Each carries a distinct dimension of Korčulan cultural life. Together, they form one of Dalmatia's oldest and most complete cultural organisations.
aboutPage.ensemblesIntro: Four ensembles, one society. Each with its own tradition and repertoire — and each integral to the experience of a Moreška performance night.
aboutPage.ctaHeadline: Every summer at the Summer Cinema, Korčula.
aboutPage.ctaBody: Twenty-four performances from May through October. Showtime 21:00.

allVignettes.title: The Dance Is Born
allVignettes.body: The earliest recorded Moreška-style performance takes place in Lérida, commemorating the Christian capture of Aragon. A ritual of mock combat between two armies is born — one that will travel the Mediterranean for centuries.

allVignettes.title: Four Centuries of Venice
allVignettes.body: Korčula begins nearly four centuries under Venetian rule. Maritime trade routes connect the island to Sicily, Southern Italy, and Spain — the same routes that will carry the sword dance tradition to its final home.

allVignettes.title: First Record in Korčula
allVignettes.body: A town journal entry dated 7 March 1666 records a Moreška performance during carnival. The dance has already taken root. While similar traditions fade across Dalmatia, Korčula holds on.

allVignettes.title: HGD Sveta Cecilija Is Founded
allVignettes.body: During the Croatian National Revival, local citizens establish the Korčula Singing Society St. Cecily — today's HGD Sveta Cecilija. Its founding statutes declare a mission to "ennoble the hearts of local youth" and defend Croatian language, folk song, and identity.

allVignettes.title: The Music Is Written Down
allVignettes.body: Croatian composer Krsto Odak and conductor Josip Svoboda create the definitive brass band score for the Moreška — the same arrangement used to this day. Seven movements, each escalating in tempo, culminating in the furious finale of the final kolap.

allVignettes.title: Revived from the Ashes
allVignettes.body: The Second World War silences the society. Costumes and swords are lost. But as the island is liberated, town barber Ivo Lozica and schoolteacher Božo Jeričević train a new troop from scratch — boys aged 12 to 17. The tradition does not die.

allVignettes.title: A New Era
allVignettes.body: Following Croatia's declaration of independence, HGD Sveta Cecilija is officially re-established under its original name and mission. The society returns to its roots: preserving the Moreška and cultivating every dimension of Korčulan cultural life.

allVignettes.title: A Protected Living Heritage
allVignettes.body: The Moreška is recognised as a protected cultural good of the Republic of Croatia. Performed twice weekly through the summer at the Summer Cinema, it remains what it has always been: the soul of Korčula, passed from father to son, one clash of iron at a time.

sectionPages.ctaBody: Twenty-four performances at the Summer Cinema, Korčula. May through October, 21:00.

moreska.heroSubtitle: Europe's last authentic war dance, performed since 1666.
moreska.lead: Of all the sword dances that once spanned the Mediterranean — from Spain to Sicily, from Venice to the Greek islands — only the Moreška of Korčula has survived intact. Not as a museum piece. As a living performance, performed in the original 17th-century Korčulan dialect, with iron swords that weigh a kilogram each and strike hard enough to produce sparks.
moreska.storyBody: Two kings. One captive maiden. Seven battles to decide her fate. The White King, Osman, arrives to reclaim his betrothed — the Bula — from the Black King, Moro, who has taken her by force. The Bula refuses to yield: "Far more pain for me is your unwanted love than the steel." What follows is the Sfida — the challenge — and then seven kolapi: a sequence of increasingly intense sword-clash figures that escalates from lyrical circling to furious final combat. The Black army is surrounded, their circle shrinking with each figure, until the Black King surrenders his sword at Osman's feet. The Bula is freed. Virtue wins. It has always won.
moreska.danceBody: The Moreška is unique in European folk tradition for its double-sword technique: every dancer wields two iron blades — the right hand strikes, the left parries. The choreography is fixed and has been passed down unchanged for generations. Each of the seven figures has a name: Rugier, Moreška, Finta, Moro in dentro, Križ, Rugier de fuorivia, and the Final Figure — executed at full speed in 4/4 time. The sound alone is worth the journey. Iron on iron, echoing off the stone walls of the old town.
moreska.costumeBody: The moreškant's attire is unlike anything else in Croatian folklore — a Roman-Oriental style tracing back to 17th-century Italian court ballet. The White army wears red; the Black army wears black. Full pleated skirts, white Roman cuffs, sashes, handkerchiefs. Ornate, martial, unmistakable.
moreska.participationBody: Moreška is traditionally performed by males from Korčulan families. Boys begin learning the steps in elementary school. A local saying captures what it means: "You are not a male if you cannot dance Moreška." Roles — including the kings and the Bula — are often passed from father to son across generations.

band.heroHeadline: The Sound of the Sword Dance
band.heroSubtitle: The musical backbone of Moreška since 1937.
band.body1: The Wind Orchestra of HGD Sveta Cecilija is the musical backbone of the Moreška. Every clash of the swords, every dramatic turn in the story, is underscored by a brass arrangement composed by Krsto Odak in 1937 — one of Croatia's foremost composers — and performed live at every performance to this day.
band.body2: But the Orchestra's role extends far beyond the summer stage. Throughout the year, the ensemble performs concert repertoire, accompanies civic celebrations, and represents Korčula at regional and national festivals. From march arrangements to contemporary Croatian compositions, the Wind Orchestra carries the musical identity of the island wherever it performs. It is the oldest continuously active musical ensemble in Southern Dalmatia.

klapa.heroSubtitle: Dalmatian a cappella, UNESCO intangible cultural heritage.
klapa.body1: Before the iron clashes, there is the voice. Klapa Sveta Cecilija opens every Moreška performance with traditional Dalmatian a cappella song — the art form of the Adriatic coast, recognised by UNESCO as intangible cultural heritage. No instruments, no amplification. Just the harmony of male voices carrying centuries of maritime soul.
klapa.body2: Klapa singing is the sound of Dalmatia at its most distilled: melancholic, warm, technically demanding, and entirely communal. To hear it in the stone courtyard of the Summer Cinema, before the swords appear, is to understand something essential about life on this island. The ensemble performs at every Moreška throughout the season and at festivals, concerts, and cultural events across the region.

choir.body1: Founded in 2023, the HGD Sveta Cecilija Choir is growing slowly but surely. After performing a series of well-received concerts and drawing members from across Korčula, the ensemble is building something the island has long been waiting for.
choir.body2: As the oldest singing society in Dalmatia, HGD Sveta Cecilija has waited a long time to bring the choir back. The choir performs choral repertoire spanning Croatian folk music, sacred works, and contemporary compositions. New members are always welcome. If you sing — or have always wanted to — the doors are open.
choir.note: Full programme and upcoming concert dates coming soon.
